REW_BEN_038.target_semantics: Multi-select benefits question: would need one share-of-employers-offering-X per option; no single positive pole exists at row level.
REW_BEN_038.verbatim_evidence: Prevalence ALL-UK vs LARGE(250+) vs SME: PMI/health insurance 18% all / 70% large / 17-18% SME; Occupational sick pay 18% all / 60% large
REW_BEN_038.flags: CONTEXT not a single prevalence: this anchor is a compendium of ~15 separate benefit prevalences (each with its own all/large/SME split), i.e. a distribution across options of a multi-select question. Usable only if split into one structured row per benefit option; several entries are themselves ranges (e.g. PMI SME 17-18%) or large-only (e.g. free eye tests 52% large). Do not collapse to one number. Note internal near-duplicates with other rows (e.g. OSP 60% large here vs 60-66% in REW_BEN_SICK_001; EAP 10% all / 61-62% large here vs 67/78/39 in REW26_WEL_EAP - conflicting EAP figures across register rows, reviewer should reconcile sources).

REW_BEN_046.target_semantics: Share of employers offering group income protection / long-term disability cover (to all employees); positive pole = Yes, offers income protection.
REW_BEN_046.verbatim_evidence: Group income protection / long-term disability: 25% all; 40% large(250+); 15% SME (offered to all employees)
REW_BEN_046.flags: Rates are on an 'offered to all employees' basis - employers offering IP to only some staff are excluded, so these slightly understate any-offer prevalence. Pairing check: SME=15, large=40; 25 is the ALL-UK rate, not the SME rate.

REW_BEN_048.target_semantics: Would describe the modal salary-replacement level among employers that offer IP (a benefit-level distribution), not a share of all employers offering anything; no prevalence pole.
REW_BEN_048.verbatim_evidence: among UK employers offering IP, 50-59% of salary is the most common replacement level (25.2%). NB 34% of employers offer no IP at all
REW_BEN_048.flags: CONTEXT not a prevalence: 50-59% is a benefit LEVEL band (percent of salary), and 25.2% is the share of the conditional base (employers already offering IP) sitting in that modal band - conditional distribution, not an all-employer target share. The 34% 'offer no IP' figure is corroboration context only and conflicts in direction of expression with REW_BEN_046 (25% offer vs 34% offer none - different sources/bases, broker-book skew per base_field). Grade B, not nationally weighted.

REW_BEN_HOL_001.target_semantics: ANNUAL-LEAVE SECTOR FLOOR (ruled): Retail/Hospitality/Logistics modal = Statutory 20 days (20+8BH = 28 = UK minimum), floor-band dist 45/32/18/4/1 (Claude derivation of the modal-20 ruling, presented at gate; floor-band DKs get real values — band-internal DK strip, flagged). SURGICAL-BAND: standard sectors byte-untouched. SUPERSEDES the global sme_large 0.553 CIPD marginal — the cohort over-weights floor sectors (84/220 = 38%) vs CIPD's economy-wide mix, so a sector-honest seed reads global 25+ ~47% (composition argument, presented for ruling). NEW-EMPLOYEE basis. TENURE explicitly NOT handled (public/charity stay 25-standard, known-approximate pending the tenure-banding mechanism — queued like location-capture).
REW_BEN_HOL_001.verbatim_evidence: 25+ days annual leave (excl bank hols), FT: 36% all; 58-59% large; 35% SME
REW_BEN_HOL_001.flags: RANGE MIDPOINT: prose says '58-59%' for large; large_pct=58.5 is the midpoint and does NOT appear verbatim - validator will not string-match it, reviewer must accept the derivation. Pairing check: SME=35, large=58.5; 36 is ALL-UK, not SME.

REW_BEN_SICK_001.target_semantics: Share of employers providing occupational sick pay above statutory SSP; positive pole = has occupational sick pay scheme (above-statutory).
REW_BEN_SICK_001.verbatim_evidence: Occupational sick pay: 18% all (Fig13); 60-66% large; 17-18% SME
REW_BEN_SICK_001.flags: TWO RANGE MIDPOINTS: large '60-66%' -> 63; SME '17-18%' -> 17.5; neither midpoint appears verbatim, so the validator will flag both - reviewer must accept the derivations. SME midpoint (17.5) sits just below all-UK (18), which is arithmetically tight but plausible given large-employer weighting. Cross-row: REW_BEN_038 compendium quotes '60% large' for the same measure (bottom of this range).

REW263_WEL_FINWELL.target_semantics: Share of employers with a documented financial wellbeing strategy/policy (beyond ad-hoc activity); positive pole = has documented strategy.
REW263_WEL_FINWELL.verbatim_evidence: Financial wellbeing strategy/policy: 15% all orgs; 53% large have-or-plan
REW263_WEL_FINWELL.flags: The 53% large figure is 'have-or-plan' - a different measure (have OR plan to have) that is NOT comparable to the 15% 'have' rate, so it is deliberately excluded from large_pct rather than mis-paired; classified all_only, not all_large. Also note sibling row REW26_WEL_FINWELL carries 64% for a broader 'any programme/activity' measure - different construct (programme activity vs documented strategy), do not reconcile them to one number.

REW26_WEL_EAP.target_semantics: Share of employers providing an Employee Assistance Programme (offered to all employees); positive pole = Yes, provides an EAP.
REW26_WEL_EAP.verbatim_evidence: EAP: 67% all employees; 78% large(250+); 39% SME (offered to all). NB HW Fig16=67% all
REW26_WEL_EAP.flags: PAIRING TRAP row: SME=39 pairs with large=78; 67 is the ALL-UK rate, NOT the SME rate. Duplicate figure in same prose ('NB HW Fig16=67% all') is consistent, no conflict. Wording '67% all employees' most plausibly means all-UK employer rate on an offered-to-all-employees basis, but could be misread as % of employees covered - reviewer should confirm employer denominator. Cross-row conflict: REW_BEN_038 compendium cites 'EAP 10% all / 61-62% large' from a different source - materially different; reviewer must pick the source of record.

REW26_WEL_SCREENING.target_semantics: Share of employers offering health screening / health assessments (offered to all employees); positive pole = Yes, offers health screening.
REW26_WEL_SCREENING.verbatim_evidence: Health screening: 35% all; 47% large(250+); 23% SME (offered to all)
REW26_WEL_SCREENING.flags: Rates are on an 'offered to all' basis - any-offer prevalence may be slightly higher. Pairing check: SME=23, large=47; 35 is ALL-UK, not SME.

REW_BEN_112.target_semantics: Would assert the share of employers whose BASE employer pension contribution falls in the 3-6% band — a distribution band over a level question ('typical contribution'), not an offer/not-offer prevalence; no binary positive pole exists.
REW_BEN_112.verbatim_evidence: "Base employer contribution: 68% of employers sit between 3% and 6% of pensionable salary (Aon Q77/78)"
REW_BEN_112.flags: CONTEXT only: 68% is the share of employers inside a 3-6% contribution RANGE — a distribution statistic for a categorical level question, likely spanning multiple answer options, so unusable as a single target share. Broker-book skew toward larger/advised employers per base_field. Grade B.

REW_BEN_PENS_EE_MAX_01.target_semantics: No share-of-employers figure exists at all — the prose gives median/quartiles/range of the max employee DC contribution RATE (a value/level distribution); nothing here can be asserted as a target share.
REW_BEN_PENS_EE_MAX_01.verbatim_evidence: "Max employee DC contribution: median 6% (p25 5%, p75 7%), range 3-12% (n=59)"
REW_BEN_PENS_EE_MAX_01.flags: CONTEXT only: all figures are contribution-rate LEVELS (median 6%, p25 5%, p75 7%, range 3-12%), not employer-prevalence percentages — the % signs denote salary percentages, not shares of employers. Tiny base (n=59) with large-employer/FTSE skew and pre-2024 vintage per base_field. Grade B.

REW_BEN_PENS_EMP_MAX_01.target_semantics: Would assert the share of employers whose MAX employer pension contribution is >=9% (or >=11%) — cumulative distribution points over a level question, not a binary offering prevalence; no single positive pole maps to the question's option set.
REW_BEN_PENS_EMP_MAX_01.verbatim_evidence: "~50% of employers offer max employer contribution 9%+; 27% offer 11%+ (Aon Q77/78)"
REW_BEN_PENS_EMP_MAX_01.flags: CONTEXT only: two OVERLAPPING cumulative thresholds (~50% at 9%+, 27% at 11%+; the 11%+ group is a subset of the 9%+ group) — these are CDF points for a banded level question, not option shares, and cannot seed a single marginal without the reviewer choosing a threshold. '~' approximate on the 50. Broker-book skew toward larger/advised employers per base_field. Grade B.

REW_FAI_STUDY_TIME_93b6ef22.target_semantics: Share of all-UK employers offering paid study leave/time; positive pole = 'yes, paid study time is provided' for the question 'Is paid study time provided for employees undertaking formal qualifications'.
REW_FAI_STUDY_TIME_93b6ef22.verbatim_evidence: "Paid study leave/time is offered by ~70% of UK employers" | "92% offer training/career-development, 75% pay professional subscriptions/fees"
REW_FAI_STUDY_TIME_93b6ef22.flags: '~70%' approximate — extracted as 70. CONSTRUCT PROXY: prose's own NOTE says 70% measures paid study LEAVE and protected paid study TIME (day-release) 'may be somewhat lower — treat as upper bound'; base_field repeats this. Usually targeted to certain employees rather than universal. The 92% (training/career-dev) and 75% (professional subscriptions) figures are OTHER benefits — do not pair with the target. Small base (~280 reward professionals, CIPD Oct 2021). DUPLICATE ANCHOR: same underlying CIPD Oct 2021 70% figure as REW_BEN_FAM_009 — two metrics will share one source number.

REW262_TIME_BEREAVEMENT.target_semantics: Share of employers offering paid bereavement leave above statutory, excluding parental bereavement; positive pole = 'yes, we offer paid bereavement leave above the statutory minimum'.
REW262_TIME_BEREAVEMENT.flags: TAXONOMY GAP: prose gives ALL (27%) AND SME (26%) but NO large rate — no all+SME base_type exists, so classified all_only on the 27% all-UK figure with sme_pct=26 recorded for the reviewer. Not sme_large (no large rate to pair). Implied large rate sits slightly above 27% given SME at 26% and SME employment weight — reviewer may wish to back-derive rather than lose the SME figure. Grade A.

REW_BEN_FAM_001.target_semantics: 33% = share of UK employers offering statutory-minimum maternity pay ONLY — this is the NEGATIVE pole of the question ('at statutory' vs 'enhanced'); the enhanced-pay positive side is the unstated complement (~67%, derived, NOT written in prose).
REW_BEN_FAM_001.verbatim_evidence: "33% of UK employers offer statutory-minimum maternity pay only; 18% offer 4-13 wks enhanced at/near full pay; 21% offer 26 wks enhanced at/near full pay then 13 wks SMP. (Private 38% statutory-only vs public 13%; public most generous.)"
REW_BEN_FAM_001.flags: POLARITY WARNING: 33% is the statutory-ONLY share — do NOT read as the share offering enhanced pay. Prose is really a partial categorical distribution (33% statutory-only, 18% enhanced 4-13wks, 21% enhanced 26wks — sums to 72%, incomplete) matching a multi-option question; only the statutory-only share is a clean single target. Sector splits present (private 38% / public 13% statutory-only) — large-generosity/sector skew. Pre-dates ERA 2025 reforms per base_field; baseline expected to shift. Grade A, n=2,000 weighted.

REW_BEN_FAM_007.target_semantics: Share of UK employers offering PAID carer's leave specifically; positive pole = 'yes, paid carer's leave provided' (the statutory Carer's Leave Act 2023 right is UNPAID, so paid provision is above-statutory).
REW_BEN_FAM_007.verbatim_evidence: "PAID carer's leave specifically offered by ~36% (RMS 2022) / 31-45% (flex 2025 support data)" | "52% of UK employers have a policy/procedure for employees to request carer's leave (75% public, 46% private, 59% voluntary; 67% large 250+ vs 33% SME)"
REW_BEN_FAM_007.flags: PAIRING/CONSTRUCT TRAP: the 67% large / 33% SME / 52% all splits measure a DIFFERENT construct — having a policy/procedure to REQUEST carer's leave (which is unpaid statutory) — NOT paid provision. Do NOT build sme_large from 33/67 for this paid-leave question. Paid figures conflict across sources: ~36% (RMS 2022) vs 31-45% range (2025 flex data, midpoint 38); extracted 36 because it is the explicit point estimate appearing verbatim (midpoint 38 does not appear in prose). '~' approximate. Sector splits (75/46/59) also belong to the request-policy construct. Grade A base, but the paid point estimate itself is older RMS 2022.

REW_BEN_FAM_009.target_semantics: Share of UK employers offering paid study leave; positive pole = 'yes, we provide paid study or exam leave'.
REW_BEN_FAM_009.verbatim_evidence: "70% of UK employers offer paid study leave" | "92% offer training/career-development, 75% pay professional subscriptions, 75% conference attendance"
REW_BEN_FAM_009.flags: Often grade/role-dependent rather than universal — prevalence of offering to ANYONE, not universal entitlement. The 92% (training/career-dev), 75% (professional subscriptions) and 75% (conference attendance) figures are OTHER L&D benefits — do not pair with the target. Private-skewed (70%) modest sample per base_field (~280 reward professionals, item bases ~212-253, CIPD Oct 2021). DUPLICATE ANCHOR: same underlying CIPD Oct 2021 70% figure as REW_FAI_STUDY_TIME_93b6ef22 — reviewer should note two metrics anchor on one source number.

REW_BEN_FAM_010.target_semantics: Share of all-UK employers offering paid volunteering leave; positive pole = 'Yes, we provide paid volunteering leave' (paid time off for voluntary/community work).
REW_BEN_FAM_010.verbatim_evidence: 30% of UK employers offer paid time off for voluntary/community work
REW_BEN_FAM_010.flags: Base per register: ~280 reward professionals (item bases ~212-253), private-skewed 70% — modest sample. Anchor's 'minority, above-statutory practice' note is qualitative context only.

REW_BEN_SICK_002.target_semantics: None usable as a target share — this is a distribution of full-pay OSP duration bands among orgs that have OSP, not a share of employers doing X; CONTEXT only.
REW_BEN_SICK_002.verbatim_evidence: OSP duration full pay: ~10% <1 month; ~40% >6 months; public sector most generous
REW_BEN_SICK_002.flags: NOT a prevalence: duration distribution (~10% under 1 month, ~40% over 6 months) with conditional denominator 'orgs with OSP', approximate '~' figures, bands do not sum to 100. Question itself asks a duration ('for how long'), not a yes/no. 'Public sector most generous' is qualitative. Route to CONTEXT.

REW_BEN_SICK_004.target_semantics: Share of orgs WITH OSP whose staff are eligible from day one, i.e. NO waiting period — the question's 'yes, there is a waiting period' side is the COMPLEMENT (~70%); the 30% attaches to the no-waiting-period pole.
REW_BEN_SICK_004.flags: CONDITIONAL DENOMINATOR: base is orgs with OSP (per register base_field), NOT all-UK employers — 'all_only' here means single-rate-over-stated-base; generator must condition on the OSP parent. Figure is approximate ('~30%'). POLARITY INVERTED vs question wording — do not seed 30% as the 'waiting period exists' share. '43% public' is a public-sector split, not extracted.

EXT_REW_GAP_009.target_semantics: Share of UK employers (for roles supporting remote work) that require a minimum number of office days; positive pole = 'yes, a mandatory office-day minimum exists' vs no requirement.
EXT_REW_GAP_009.verbatim_evidence: 65% of UK employers require a minimum number of office days
EXT_REW_GAP_009.flags: Only the headline 65% is extracted. The day-count split (3 days 48%, 2 days 32%, 4 days 14%, 1 day 8%) is CONDITIONAL on the 65% ('Among those') — never treat those as marginals. '5-10 days (26%)' by-month and '14% of hybrid employers plan to introduce/increase' are context/forward-looking. Question is frequency-shaped ('how often'); 65% answers only the any-requirement split, with the conditional distribution as shape context. Strong base: n=2,050, weighted, nationally representative (CIPD/YouGov 2025).

OT_04_b14623a6.target_semantics: Share of UK employers paying a shift premium for unsocial hours; positive pole = 'Yes, shift premiums are paid'.
OT_04_b14623a6.verbatim_evidence: 63% of employers pay a shift premium
OT_04_b14623a6.flags: LARGE-SKEW BASE: register base_field says large-employer/FTSE skew, answered-only n, pre-2024 vintage — prose says 'employers' with no size qualifier, so treat 63% as large-skewed rather than a clean all-UK rate. Grade B. Premium-structure parenthetical (fixed-rate most common; some % of hourly) is qualitative context.

PROP_634adacd.target_semantics: None usable as a target share — 3% is a median pay-award LEVEL (a value employers award), not a share of employers doing X; CONTEXT only.
PROP_634adacd.verbatim_evidence: Median expected basic pay increase 3% overall, across all sectors (private/public/voluntary)
PROP_634adacd.flags: NOT a prevalence: 3% is the median EXPECTED basic pay increase (forward-looking level/value). Seeding it as an employer share would be a category error. Route to CONTEXT for the salary-increase question's answer distribution.

REW26_WEL_FINWELL.target_semantics: Share of UK employers offering a financial wellbeing programme/activity (broad any-programme measure); positive pole = 'Yes, we offer a financial wellbeing programme'.
REW26_WEL_FINWELL.flags: Register relabel history: ruled 2026-07-16 that this anchor is the broad 'programme/activity' measure (previous 'policy/strategy' mislabel; audit mis-map OVERTURNED) — anchor attachment is correct as-is, but source wave still to be tightened to the exact CIPD survey carrying S29. Base described only as 'mixed UK', not a clean all-UK denominator. Broad/generous definition — expect the rate to run high vs stricter policy measures.

REW26_WEL_STRATEGY.target_semantics: Share of UK employers with a stand-alone documented wellbeing strategy; positive pole = 'Yes, a documented wellbeing strategy exists' (the question's review-frequency tail is not covered by this rate).
REW26_WEL_STRATEGY.flags: Size figures in prose are band EXTREMES, not SME/large(250+) rates: '75% at 1000+' (subset of large, would overstate a 250+ rate) and '21% at 2-49' (excludes 50-249, would understate an SME rate) — deliberately NOT extracted into large_pct/sme_pct to avoid band mismatch; use as size-gradient context/shape only. Only the 57% all-UK rate is a clean target share.

EXT_REW_GAP_004.target_semantics: Share of employers operating a long-service award scheme; positive pole = Yes (operates a scheme).
EXT_REW_GAP_004.flags: Base_field warns of large-employer/FTSE skew and answered-only n, so this single rate likely overstates SME prevalence — treated as all-UK only because no size split exists in prose; pre-2024 vintage; grade B.

PAYTR_01_42eae7ec.target_semantics: Share of UK employers whose job adverts TYPICALLY include a salary or salary range; positive pole = Yes (salary/range typically included).
PAYTR_01_42eae7ec.flags: Two candidate cuts in prose: ~40% 'always' vs 53% 'always-or-sometimes'. Chose 40 because the question says 'typically', which is closer to 'always' than to 'sometimes'; reviewer may prefer 53. The 40 is written as approximate ('~40%'). Same underlying stat as REW262_GOV_PAYINADVERTS, where I chose the 53 cut for its unqualified 'do you include' wording — confirm the pair is intentional.

PROP_10d1211d.target_semantics: Share of employers that ran a disability pay gap analysis in the past year; positive pole = Yes (ran the analysis).
PROP_10d1211d.verbatim_evidence: 18% of UK employers ran disability pay gap analysis in year to Oct 2023 (27% of large 250+ employers had done so vs 9% of SMEs
PROP_10d1211d.flags: Pairing-trap shape: 18 is the ALL-UK rate, NOT the SME rate — blend pair is SME=9 / large=27. The trailing '46% of large had NOT, 28% didn't know' are complement/don't-know context, not prevalence targets (27+46+28=101 due to rounding, large don't-know share is big). 'No legal requirement currently' is qualitative context only.

PROP_930043cc.target_semantics: Share of UK employers that carry out ethnicity pay gap analysis; positive pole = Yes (carries out the analysis).
PROP_930043cc.flags: The '(10% share)' is the subset who SHARE/disclose results, not the carry-out prevalence — do not use as target. 18 carry-out + 56 have-not = 74, so ~26% unaccounted (presumably don't know/planning). 'Main barrier company size' is qualitative context; base is size-skewed in behaviour but no numeric size split given.

REW262_GOV_PAYINADVERTS.target_semantics: Share of UK employers that include pay or a pay range in job adverts at least sometimes; positive pole = Yes (includes pay/range).
REW262_GOV_PAYINADVERTS.flags: Two candidate cuts: ~40% 'always' vs 53% 'always-or-sometimes'. Chose 53 because this question is an unqualified 'Do you include...', so any inclusion counts; the stricter 40 ('always', written as approximate '~40%') is the alternative. Same underlying stat as PAYTR_01_42eae7ec, where I chose 40 for its 'typically' wording — reviewer should confirm the two metrics are meant to sit at different cuts.

REW263_GOV_UKPAYTRANS.target_semantics: Share of UK employers publishing salary ranges always-or-sometimes — a binarized cut of a categorical 'how would you describe your pay-transparency position' question; positive pole = the option-side(s) indicating ranges are published (always/sometimes) vs not.
REW263_GOV_UKPAYTRANS.verbatim_evidence: 53% publish ranges always/sometimes; only 16% publish both ranges+specifics
REW263_GOV_UKPAYTRANS.flags: Question is categorical ('How would you describe...'), not yes/no, so 53 only works as a target if the option set binarizes cleanly into publishes-ranges vs not — reviewer must confirm the option map; if it cannot be binarized, downgrade to CONTEXT. The 16% ('publish both ranges+specifics') is a stricter top-band cut, usable only for a top-option target; 'only 16%' is rhetorical phrasing. Overlaps the same underlying 53% stat as PAYTR_01/REW262.

REW_FAI_079.target_semantics: Share of UK employers that carry out gender pay gap analysis; positive pole = Yes (conducts the analysis).
REW_FAI_079.verbatim_evidence: 44% carry out (30% always+14% sometimes share); mandatory 250+; public sector 52%; 79% of <250 had NOT
REW_FAI_079.flags: NOT sme_large despite size info: the large rate is given only as 'mandatory 250+' (de jure ~100%, no number in prose) and the sub-250 rate only negatively ('79% of <250 had NOT', implying ~21% positive — NOT stated verbatim, so cannot be output). Parenthetical 30+14 decomposes the 44 into always/sometimes SHARING of results, not separate carry-out rates. 'Public sector 52%' is a sector split, context only. Large-vs-SME behaviour differs hugely here — flag for blend logic since mandatory 250+ means the 44 all-UK rate masks a near-ceiling large rate.

REW_FAI_089.target_semantics: Recommended target: share of ALL UK employers publishing salary ranges on external job adverts always-or-sometimes = 53% — POSITIVE-pole figure mapping to the 'Some roles'+'All roles' side of the ruled ordering. The coexisting 47% ('never/plans') is the NEGATIVE POLE complement (maps to 'No'; 53+47=100, internally consistent). The ~40% 'always' figure is the 'All roles'-only share and is approximate as written.
REW_FAI_089.verbatim_evidence: "Publish salary ranges in external job adverts: ~40% always; 53% always-or-sometimes; 47% never/plans". Note '~40%' carries a tilde (approximate); 53 and 47 are exact as written.
REW_FAI_089.ordering_needed: Ruled ordering EXISTS in ruled_orderings.json: ['No','Some roles','All roles'], no positive_from → default lean = {'No'}, positive = Some roles + All roles. The 53% target matches this default split exactly, so NO new key is needed if 53 is ruled in. Using ~40% 'always' instead would require positive_from='All roles'.
REW_FAI_089.flags: MARGINAL CANDIDATE with a deliberate-pick requirement: three coexisting figures for one axis — 53 (recommended, matches default ruled split), 47 (NEGATIVE POLE — never report as positive; also bundles 'plans to' intenders into the No side), ~40 (approximate, All-roles-only, needs positive_from change). Same CIPD PPT 2024 base as EQUALPAYAUDIT: all-UK label but large-skewed composition (57% large 250+). No SME/large split figures → all_only, no pairing-trap exposure.

REW_BEN_HOL_003.target_semantics: NEGATIVE POLE — the 44.1% is the share of UK employers that do NOT offer holiday trading at all, i.e. the lean/'No' rung share, NOT the positive target. The polarity guard must invert: positive pole (any of 'Yes - sell only' / 'Yes - buy only' / 'Yes - both buy and sell options are available') = 100 - 44.1 = 55.9% (derived, not verbatim). Do not report 44.1 as the offer share.
REW_BEN_HOL_003.verbatim_evidence: "44.1% of UK employers do NOT offer holiday trading at all"; secondary: "18.8% allow buy/sell of up to 5 days". Distractor: "up from 35.8% prior year" (historical trend, do not use).
REW_BEN_HOL_003.ordering_needed: Ruled ordering EXISTS: No < Yes - sell only < Yes - buy only < Yes - both buy and sell options are available (ruled 2026-07-15/16). No positive_from → default second rung: lean = {No}, which matches the 44.1% figure exactly. No new ordering needed. Distribution across the three Yes rungs is not given by the source (the marginal can only pin the No-vs-any-Yes split).
REW_BEN_HOL_003.flags: POLARITY TRAP instance (the FAM_001-style case the prompt warns about): source figure counts the none share — flag NEGATIVE POLE. Grade B convenience sample: n=626 incl. broker's own clients, skews larger/benefits-engaged, explicitly 'Not UK-representative' — large-skew flag despite nominal all-sizes base. 18.8% is a days-cap detail (up-to-5-days) belonging to HOL_006-type semantics, not this metric's pole. 'Don't know' is is_na.

REW263_REC_IMPACT.target_semantics: Marginal would assert the share of UK orgs answering YES to 'Do you measure the impact of recognition on engagement or retention?' (yes_no question; positive side = Yes/measures impact). The recorded 56.4% is the 'do NOT track' share — NEGATIVE POLE, explicitly. CRITICAL: simple inversion (100−56.4=43.6) is NOT the Yes share, because the 17.7% 'want to but do not know how' group is also No-side; the true Yes share is ~2% formal + 16.2% informal = 18.2% — DERIVED, not verbatim.
REW263_REC_IMPACT.verbatim_evidence: "only ~2% formally track ROI on gifting/recognition (clear metrics); 16.2% informal indicators; 56.4% do NOT track; 17.7% want to but do not know how". Note ~2% is written with a tilde (approximate). Categories sum to 92.3%, leaving ~7.7% unaccounted.
REW263_REC_IMPACT.ordering_needed: Not in ruled_orderings.json but the DB question is yes_no (Yes/No) — obvious axis, lean side = 'No'. No new ordering key needed; what IS needed is a polarity/derivation ruling on the target figure.
REW263_REC_IMPACT.flags: POLARITY TRAP LIVE (FAM_001 pattern): 56.4% is the negative-pole figure — never report as the positive target. Standard polarity-guard inversion is UNSAFE here (43.6 wrongly counts the 17.7% intenders as Yes); David must rule between authorising the derived 18.2 (2+16.2, not verbatim in register) or rejecting for context. Sample quality: n=85 self-selected vendor audience, grade C, directional only. Secondary CIPD 2023 figures (89% offer some recognition; 34% evaluate reward-strategy effectiveness) are DIFFERENT constructs — do not substitute as this metric's figure.

REW263_TIME_FERTLEAVE.target_semantics: Share of ALL UK employers (n=2,023, nationally representative) providing PAID time off for fertility appointments/treatment. POSITIVE-pole figure (22% is the provide-paid side, not a negative/none share). Maps to the TOP rung 'Paid dedicated leave' only — NOT to the default positive side of the existing ordering. The middle rung 'Unpaid/flexible' has its own candidate figure (27% flexibility of working hours) and the no-support share is 28%.
REW263_TIME_FERTLEAVE.verbatim_evidence: "22% of UK employers provide PAID time off to attend fertility appointments/manage treatment"; supporting distribution: "27% provide flexibility of working hours", "49% provide some kind of fertility support overall; 28% provide none".
REW263_TIME_FERTLEAVE.ordering_needed: Ruled ordering EXISTS: No < Unpaid/flexible < Paid dedicated leave (ruled 2026-07-15/16). No positive_from key, so default second-rung applies: lean = {No}, positive = {Unpaid/flexible, Paid dedicated leave} (~49%-any-support semantics). Emitting a marginal at 22% ALSO needs a positive_from ruling = 'Paid dedicated leave' (top rung), otherwise the guard will read 22% against the wrong (any-support) pole. Alternatively rule the marginal at the 49% any-support level — David's call.
REW263_TIME_FERTLEAVE.flags: Grade A nationally representative — strong candidate. Basis nuance: source counts 'paid time off to attend appointments/manage treatment', question asks 'dedicated paid fertility-treatment leave (separate from sick/annual)' — the 22% may include non-dedicated paid time off, mild offered-basis mismatch. Multiple coexisting figures (22/27/27/49/28) — only 22% matches the question's top rung; 27% policy-prevalence and 34%/19% public/private splits are distractors. DB has an is_na 'Not applicable' option. Sector splits available, no size splits quoted.

REW_BEN_FAM_008.target_semantics: Share of all-UK employers offering paid bereavement/compassionate leave. POSITIVE-pole figure (86% = offer it). Positive side of the option set is 'Yes - paid leave is provided' and plausibly 'Partially paid (case-by-case)'; negative side is 'Unpaid only' + 'No specific provision'. The source's 'offer it' basis likely counts ANY paid provision, so 86% may straddle the Yes-paid and Partially-paid rungs rather than being the Yes-paid rung alone.
REW_BEN_FAM_008.verbatim_evidence: "86% of UK employers offer it" (referent: "Paid bereavement/compassionate leave is near-universal"). Distractors NOT to use: "up from 71% in 2018" (historical), "25+ days holiday (82%)" (different benefit).
REW_BEN_FAM_008.ordering_needed: NO entry in ruled_orderings.json — a marginal needs a NEW ruled ordering. Obvious axis: No specific provision < Unpaid only < Partially paid (case-by-case) < Yes - paid leave is provided, but the 4-rung set differs from ruled siblings FAM_007/009/010 (3 rungs), so it cannot inherit. Also needs a positive_from ruling on whether 'Partially paid (case-by-case)' sits on the positive side (recommended, to match the source's any-paid-offer basis). Lean side = {No specific provision, Unpaid only}. 'Don't know' is is_na.
REW_BEN_FAM_008.flags: Grade A but modest base: ~280 reward professionals (item bases ~212-253), CIPD Oct 2021, private-skewed 70% — older and smaller than the n=1,059 benefits-focus survey. Rung-straddle risk: 86% is an any-paid-offer figure against a question that splits fully-paid vs case-by-case. Two distractor numbers in anchor (71% historical, 82% different benefit).

REW262_GOV_EQUALPAYAUDIT.target_semantics: Share of ALL UK employers (CIPD Pay, Performance and Transparency 2024, n=832) that ran an equal-pay audit in the last 12 months = 35%. POSITIVE-pole figure (audit-side) — no inversion needed; lean side = 'No'. Basis caution: 'in last 12m' maps to the 'Annually'+'More than annually' side of the ruled ordering, NOT to any-audit-including-'Ad hoc'.
REW262_GOV_EQUALPAYAUDIT.verbatim_evidence: "Equal-pay audit in last 12m: 35% of employers" — the entire anchor; single clean figure, no range.
REW262_GOV_EQUALPAYAUDIT.ordering_needed: Ruled ordering EXISTS in ruled_orderings.json: ['No','Ad hoc','Annually','More than annually'], no positive_from → default second-rung behaviour puts lean = {'No'} and positive = Ad hoc + Annually + More than annually. Emitting 35% at that default treats it as an any-audit rate, but the figure's last-12m basis matches positive_from='Annually' semantics — David should either add positive_from='Annually' or explicitly accept the mismatch.
REW262_GOV_EQUALPAYAUDIT.flags: MARGINAL CANDIDATE. Single all-base figure, no SME/large split given → all_only, no pairing-trap exposure. Sample composition large-skewed within an all-UK label (large 250+ = 57%, SME = 43% of n=832) — note for weighting. The question also asks 'how often' but the anchor supports only the ran-in-last-12m split, not the Ad hoc/Annually/More-than-annually mix.

CAR_STATUS_01.target_semantics: RULED 0.35 (grade-based figure, matches question wording 'status or grade-based'); 37 any-offer REJECTED. POSITIVE share.
CAR_STATUS_01.verbatim_evidence: "~37% of UK employers offer it (2% to all staff, 35% dependent on grade/seniority/role)" — 37 = any-offer headline; 35 = grade-dependent-only alternative; both verbatim in register.
CAR_STATUS_01.ordering_needed: Not in ruled_orderings.json. Obvious binary yes/no axis: lean side = 'No', positive = 'Yes'. No multi-rung ordering to rule, but a trivial No<Yes entry (or yes/no default) must exist for the generator if emitted.
CAR_STATUS_01.flags: RULING NEEDED 37-vs-35: question wording ('status or grade-based') vs anchor headline (any-offer). Base modest and dated: CIPD Oct 2021, ~280 reward professionals, transport-benefit item base n=217, private-skewed. Do NOT use the car-allowance (~47%) or car-ownership-scheme (~8%) figures — different constructs in the same anchor sentence.

PAYTR_02_131bd412.target_semantics: RULED 0.40 = 'always' only (unconditional-rung principle); 'sometimes' 28% is lean-side. POSITIVE share.
PAYTR_02_131bd412.verbatim_evidence: "internal ~40% always publish; 32% never" (full anchor: "Pay bands/ranges visible to employees: internal ~40% always publish; 32% never"). ~40 recorded as 40 — tilde-approximate as written, not a range midpoint.
PAYTR_02_131bd412.ordering_needed: NO entry in ruled_orderings.json. Obvious yes/no axis: lean = {No}, positive = {Yes} — but generator convention gives even binaries a ruled entry (cf. REW_BEN_HOL_004, EXT_REW_GAP_004), so promoting this likely needs a new two-rung entry No < Yes, plus the Yes-mapping ruling above (always vs always-or-sometimes).
PAYTR_02_131bd412.flags: Binary-vs-trichotomy mismatch: source has always/sometimes/never structure, DB question is plain Yes/No — the ~28% residual is the ruling's main decision. Large-skew composition: sample is 57% large (250+) vs 43% SME (n=832), far above UK population share, though quoted rates are all-UK blended with no separate SME/large rates (so NOT sme_large — nothing to blend). Approximate figure (~40%). Related-but-distinct metric REW_FAI_089 (external adverts, same source) has its own figures — do not cross-wire internal vs external transparency.

_holds_ruled_2026_07_16.REW_BEN_REM_PAY_001: PROMOTED 2026-07-18 — complete 6-rung ordering ruled (4 ordinal + NA/DK structural skips); emits at 0.64. Hold released.
_holds_ruled_2026_07_16.REW_BEN_FAM_011: HOLD — 26% is a middle rung, not a pole; paid rung unquantified; understates by unknown
_holds_ruled_2026_07_16.REW263_BEN_NEURO: HOLD — source self-disclaims UK-representativeness; disqualifying, no emit-with-caveat

_principle_unconditional_rung: RULED 2026-07-16: for binary 'do you do X' questions sourced from always/sometimes/never or regular/ad-hoc data, positive = the UNCONDITIONAL rung only; the intermittent rung is lean-side. Seeding 'sometimes' as positive would mark orgs as doing something they do inconsistently.

REW_BEN_REM_PAY_001.source: CIPD "Flexible and hybrid working practices in 2025" (July 2025, ref 8909). YouGov, n=2,050 UK HR professionals, weighted/representative.
REW_BEN_REM_PAY_001.target_semantics: Share of all-UK employers who keep base pay protected when an employee moves office->remote. Positive side = 'Base pay is protected'; lean side = the adjusted options ('Base pay may be adjusted over time', 'Base pay is adjusted immediately'). all_pct=64 is the POSITIVE share (employers who 'have not and do not intend to' reduce). The companion 4% figure is NEGATIVE POLE - it counts employers who HAVE reduced pay and/or benefits - and must never be reported as the positive target. Note the survey basis is reduction of 'pay and/or benefits' for predominantly-home-working staff (behavioural, wider than base pay), while the question asks how BASE PAY is treated as policy.
REW_BEN_REM_PAY_001.verbatim_evidence: 64 from "64% have not and do not intend to". Companion figures: "only 4% of UK employers have reduced pay and/or benefits for predominantly-home-working staff" (NEGATIVE POLE); "11% plan to in future"; "13% have instead provided a contribution toward home-working costs".
REW_BEN_REM_PAY_001.ruled: 2026-07-18: promoted off hold at 0.64; full 4-rung ordering ruled (varies=ordinal second-leanest); positive_from='Base pay is protected'.

_polarity_guard_completion: RULED 2026-07-18: explicit structured 'polarity' field takes precedence over the target_semantics substring heuristic. The substring read of adjacent prose was a soft form of the banned prose-inference (FAI_089 near-miss: companion-pole mention almost inverted a positive figure). Heuristic retained as legacy fallback only.
_principle_scope_2026_07_18: Unconditional-rung principle SCOPE: frequency-sourced binaries (always/sometimes/never, regular/ad-hoc) take the unconditional rung only — stops 'sometimes X' counting as 'does X'. It does NOT reach any-offer-sourced ordinals (e.g. FAM_008): case-by-case paid provision IS paid provision; such bases distribute across all offering rungs via positive_from.

REW_PAY_005.target_semantics: Ideal pay market position — peaked on On-Market (median-targeters dominate). Seeds over ANSWERING orgs only (N/A-Don't-know option stripped per standing rule; 28 answers n-excluded). STRATEGY-CONFIG metric (Diff 4): never pools, no verdict — reshape only, ruled 2026-07-18 ①.

EXT_REW_GAP_010.target_semantics: Remote-work location permission — Anywhere-UK dominant, international a thin tail. Don't-know stripped (4 answers n-excluded). ANCHORED BUT NON-DIRECTIONAL (ruled 2026-07-18 ②): no honest better/worse pole; renders prevalence, no verdict.

REW265_PAY_RANGEMAX.target_semantics: Range-max policy RECOMPUTED (r3s3 coupling): conditioned on the PER-ORG have-ranges set from REW_PAY_001's maturity assignment (strict Yes). Members split lump/continue/frozen 40/20/40; non-members answer 'No formal ranges'. PER-ORG COHERENCE IS THE HARD ASSERT (zero have-ranges!=Yes with a policy answer) — the pay-structure-family guard. Durable by construction: RANGEMAX is orderless-nominal (engine skip, never re-paired) and REW_PAY_001 re-derives hash-deterministically. Aggregate here is the freeze-gate tier-2b cover (~45/22/22/11).

PROP_fe1a29ec.target_semantics: External benchmarking on the MATURITY GRADIENT (r3s2 pilot, ruled): formal% keyed per-org on HR_Maturity - Basic 15 / Developing 60 / Advanced 90 (EST, D. Whitfield). Replaces the Diff-15 flat 60/30/10 cohort-centre placeholder. Remainder informal:none shifts by band (2:1/3:1/4:1 - informal higher at lower maturity, derivation presented for ruling). Maturity-not-size keying per David's principle; a small-but-Advanced org correctly reads high.

maturity_anchors.positive_option: Yes - formal/structured (survey data, provider, market analysis)
remainder_options: Informally only (job adverts, ad hoc, online, ChatGPT)

_diff15_notes.PENSION_TYPE: re-frozen tier-1 at DC .95/DB .0318/Hybrid .0182 (None option REMOVED — AE legal mandate; ruled ③); lives in frozen_targets.json not here
_diff15_notes.PAYCOMMS: REW265_PAY_PAYCOMMS redesigned single_select->by-level matrix, EST verdict-suppressed; per-level Letter+conversation 90/90/90/80/70/50/20 in diff15 derivation config
_diff15_notes.PENLEAVEGAP: REW264_PEN_PENLEAVEGAP retired (class 6) — unpaid leave has no pensionable-pay basis

REW_FAI_128.target_semantics: Top-performer market pricing on the maturity gradient (r3s3): Basic 5 / Developing 25 / Advanced 70 -> cohort ~44%% Yes (was 59%% engine-flat). No sector gate (any sector can be maturity-mature). Remainder No:Planned 8:1/8:1/3:1 (Planned share rises with maturity among non-doers — derivation presented for ruling). Stays verdict-suppressed EST.

REW_PAY_001.target_semantics: Formal pay ranges (have-ranges) on the maturity gradient (r3s3): Basic 5 / Developing 30 / Advanced 90 -> cohort ~55%% Yes (supersedes the Diff-15 flat 40%% conditioning). STRICT-Yes defines the have-ranges set for the RANGEMAX coupling (Partially does NOT count — a partially-ranged org answers 'No formal ranges' on range-max; presented for ruling). Remainder Partially:No 1:3/1:1/2:1 (partial adoption rises with maturity among non-full adopters). Stays verdict-suppressed EST.

REW_PAY_TIPS_EXIST_7c80c508.target_semantics: Tips/service charges WITHIN THE SECTOR SCOPE only (r3s4): sector_scopes.json hides the metric entirely outside Hospitality; the 199 out-of-scope answers (the impossible 67.5%% all-org yes) are DELETED, not NA'd. Within the 15 hospitality orgs: 80/20 operate/don't. Freeze tier-2b checks over in-scope answers (all answers are in-scope by construction post-clean).

REW_INC_103.target_semantics: Bonus-eligible %% on the SECTOR-KEYED gradient (r3sw2 ruled): bonus EXISTS Commercial 90 / Charity 20 / Public 5; breadth among havers Commercial broad (75%%+ modal) / Charity moderate (~50) / Public narrow (frontline-perf). Corrects the Diff-13 absolute charity/public exclusion — low-but-nonzero is the reality. Band splits are my derivation of David's two-stage figures, presented at the gate. within_band=latent (coherence spine; hash stays maturity-only per the Step-2 ruling scope). Self-heals re-tag drift by re-derivation.

_coherence_pairs.note: operates-LTIP=Yes must be EXACTLY the orgs with any LTI-by-level eligibility (r3sw2 ruled, range-max pattern)
clawback-Yes must be a SUBSET of the bonus-exists set (r3sw5 ruled) — you cannot claw back a bonus you don't pay; prevents a future INC_103 reseed silently re-breaking the conditioning

PMI composition is CONDITIONED on PMI-exists (r3sw8 N/A-exclusion pilot): every org answering the composition multi-select must tick PMI in the benefits inventory — the applicable base IS the conditioning set; prevents a future REW_BEN_038 reseed silently widening the graph past the PMI-haver cohort
share-plan participation is answered only by plan operators (r3sw11 ruled): substantive answers ⊆ the any-plan set
SAYE discount exists only where SAYE does (r3sw11 ruled — the 12 SIP-only 20%-max artifacts repaired): substantive ⊆ the SAYE side
SIP elements exist only where a SIP does (r3sw11 ruled — 16 SAYE-only fabrications + 6 operator no-SIP contradictions repaired): substantive ⊆ the SIP side
share-capital has ONE authority — the parent's no-shares option (r3sw11 ruled, one-authority principle): EMICSOP substantive ⊆ parent share-capital set; the child's own N/A must never diverge again
EWA fee model exists only where EWA runs (r3sw12 ruled: OFFER-OR-PILOT grain — pilots are live schemes with real fee models): substantive ⊆ the 77-org offer-or-pilot set
EWA withdrawal caps exist only where EWA runs (r3sw12 ruled: OFFER-OR-PILOT grain): substantive ⊆ the 77-org offer-or-pilot set
PMI by-level eligibility is CONDITIONED on PMI-exists (r3sw14): only PMI-havers hold matrix rows — same parent as premium/composition; prevents a reseed re-widening the base past the haver cohort
deferral period/vehicle is CONDITIONED on the org deferring (r3sw16): child answerers ⊆ the parent's deferrer set (listing/FS re-anchor) — level 1 of the two-level chain
a deferrer must have a bonus to defer (r3sw16): parent-Yes set ⊆ bonus-havers (grandparent != None) — level 2 of the chain; forces the 36 no-bonus-but-defer absurdities to stay fixed
IP waiting-period is CONDITIONED on IP-exists (r3sw17): child answerers ⊆ the 046 IP-haver set (046 is the ruled IP authority, not the 038 inventory tick)
IP salary-replacement is CONDITIONED on IP-exists (r3sw17): child ⊆ 046 IP-haver set — the badly over-seeded child (145 substantive) repaired to the 80
group-IP rehab usage is CONDITIONED on IP-exists (r3sw17): child ⊆ 046 IP-haver set

parent_value_in: Combination of enhanced sick pay and SSP
_coherence_pairs.note: OSP eligibility-rules governance is CONDITIONED on OSP-exists (r3sw17, r3sw13-routed): child ⊆ OSP-havers (SICK_001 enhanced/combination) — SEPARATE parent from IP; subset not equality (a governance question — not every OSP-haver documents)

PMI eligibility-rules is CONDITIONED on PMI-exists (r3sw19): child answerers ⊆ the 153 PMI-haver set — same PMI-family authority as composition/by-level/premium; the redesigned 3-option single-select ('Not offered' dropped via conditioning)

PMI premium is CONDITIONED on PMI-exists (r3sw20): child answerers ⊆ the PMI-haver set — now guardable because Thornbridge (the demo org with genuine premiums) is a real haver, closing the last unguarded family child (the r3sw6/15 documented-exception is gone)

PMI cost-share/excess is CONDITIONED on PMI-exists (r3sw22, 6th PMI-family child): every org reporting an excess ⊆ the 154 PMI-havers. SUBSET not equality (ruled) — 84 havers answered N/A (no excess detail), rendered over the 70 excess-specifiers; the 26 non-haver excesses cleared. answerer_only excludes 'Not applicable'

dental funding split is CONDITIONED on dental-exists (r3sw23): funding-answerers ⊆ the sector-gated REW_BEN_038 'Dental cover' tick set (~11, perk-concentrated). 038 Dental-tick is the parent (like PMI); both mis-seeded signals aligned. EST grade-C, verdict-suppressed
CI cover-level is CONDITIONED on CI-exists (r3sw24): level-answerers ⊆ the aligned 52 CI-havers (038 CI-tick, dedicated-signal-wins) — level 1 of the group-risk chain
CI rides inside the group-risk bundle (r3sw24): every CI-haver is an IP-haver (52 ⊆ 80) — level 2; locks the bundle so a reseed can't create a CI-haver who isn't an IP-haver

REW26_BEN_PENSION_TYPE.source: TPR Occupational DB landscape 2025 (data 31 Mar 2025) — 200 public-service schemes, 19.78m members, open to new members by statute; CARE = standard DB (no separate TPR class)
REW26_BEN_PENSION_TYPE.target_semantics: PUBLIC-SECTOR CARVE-OUT (r3sw3 ruled, TPR grade A): public-band new joiners get CARE=DB 95 / DC 5; NO _default — commercial/charity bands are byte-untouched (surgical-band contract), staying at the frozen national 95/3/2. Tier-1 RE-FREEZE at the post-carve national aggregate (G7 baseline re-established, EAP precedent). CONSTRUCT FLAG: TPR counts schemes/members, Lumi asks org-offer — immaterial for the public band (statutory participation ~= offer). New-employee basis per the standing rule.

REW264_HLT_VIRTUALGP.source: CIPD Health & Wellbeing 2025 (Mar-Apr 2025 fieldwork, YouGov n=1,101 employers), large-private 250+ band
REW264_HLT_VIRTUALGP.target_semantics: VIRTUAL GP RE-ANCHOR (r3sw7, ruled): total provision SOURCED grade A/B — CIPD H&W 2025 large-private band: 54% all-staff / 69% any-form -> No ~31%. BASIS CORRECTION: the old 77% No was anchored all-sizes-incl-micro; the cohort is 250-999 = the large-private band. Standalone-vs-Via-PMI-only split EST (paywalled decomposition), CONSTRAINED by the sourced fact virtual GP (54%) > PMI (40%) for large-private -> Via-PMI-only is a minority (11). SIZE GRADIENT RECORDED NOT GATED (SME 23% / large 54% on register as reference; cohort uniformly one band, seeded flat cohort-centre). VERDICT RE-EARNS: first benefits metric back through the Diff-14 path — unbenchmarked lifts, provision verdict renders.

REW265_BEN_PMICOMP.source: Insurer product structure: Bupa/AXA Health/Aviva/Vitality corporate PMI tiers + broker guides (Drewberry, myTribe, Usay) — option list and standard-vs-optional classification sourced; per-option incidence EST, structure-anchored (standard-adjacent high, add-on low)
REW265_BEN_PMICOMP.terminal: None of these — core cover only
REW265_BEN_PMICOMP.base: PMI-holding orgs = REW_BEN_038 'Private Medical Insurance (PMI)' tickers (n=130 seed)
REW265_BEN_PMICOMP.target_semantics: PMI COVERAGE-COMPOSITION REDESIGN (r3sw8): replaces REW263_BEN_PMIMH (single-select, N/A 56.8% distorting the distribution). Multi-select over the APPLICABLE BASE ONLY — conditioned on PMI-exists, no N/A option, n = PMI-havers by construction (the N/A-not-on-graph pilot). Prevalences are INDEPENDENT per-option shares of PMI-havers (won't sum to 100) — the tier-2d incidence gate holds each at max(5pp, 1/n). 4 UNIVERSALS ASSUMED STANDARD, not benchmarked (in-patient, digital GP, cancer cover, basic MH support line — ~90%+ everywhere, stated in help_text). Terminal 'None of these — core cover only' is derived-exclusive (zero-pick orgs stay in n). VERDICT-SUPPRESSED (EST incidence); per-option re-earn path when by-option incidence is sourced.
